Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → Large language models and NLP → LLM fundamentals

Lesson 1 of 3

LLM fundamentals

Pre-training objectives, tokenization, context windows, and the emergent capabilities that appear at scale.

30-40 min AI/ML flow Large language models and NLP 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

LLMs are reshaping software engineering. Understanding their capabilities and limitations is essential for building reliable AI-powered applications.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. How LLMs are trained Pre-training learns language patterns from trillions of tokens2. Tokenization and context Token count determines memory usage and computational cost per request3. Capabilities and limitations Hallucination is inherent to probabilistic generation and requires mitigation strategies

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing LLMs are reshaping software engineering. Understanding their capabilities and limitations is essential for building reliable AI-powered applications. 2. How LLMs are trained Pre-training learns language patterns from trillions of tokens3. Tokenization and context Token count determines memory usage and computational cost per request4. Capabilities and limitations Hallucination is inherent to probabilistic generation and requires mitigation strategies
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

LLMs are reshaping software engineering. Understanding their capabilities and limitations is essential for building reliable AI-powered applications.

What to say first

  • Can explain the training pipeline from pre-training through RLHF.
  • Understands tokenization and its impact on model behavior and cost.
  • Knows the main failure modes of LLMs and mitigation approaches.
2

Study section

How LLMs are trained

Next-token prediction on massive text corpora, followed by RLHF alignment, produces models that follow instructions and reason about novel tasks.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Pre-training learns language patterns from trillions of tokens
  • Instruction tuning and RLHF align the model with human preferences
  • Emergent abilities (reasoning, code generation) appear at sufficient scale
3

Study section

Tokenization and context

BPE, SentencePiece, and tiktoken break text into subword tokens that define the model vocabulary and context window limits.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Token count determines memory usage and computational cost per request
  • Context window size limits how much information the model can use at once
  • Tokenization artifacts can cause surprising behavior with numbers, code, and rare words

Code example

Count tokens and estimate cost with tiktoken

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")  # GPT-4 tokenizer

text = "Retrieval-augmented generation grounds LLM responses in external knowledge."
tokens = enc.encode(text)

print(f"Text: {text}")
print(f"Token count: {len(tokens)}")
print(f"Tokens: {[enc.decode([t]) for t in tokens]}")

# Estimate API cost (input tokens at $0.01/1K)
cost_per_1k = 0.01
estimated_cost = len(tokens) * cost_per_1k / 1000
print(f"Estimated cost: ${estimated_cost:.6f}")
4

Study section

Capabilities and limitations

LLMs excel at pattern completion but struggle with factual accuracy, arithmetic, and maintaining consistency over long outputs.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Hallucination is inherent to probabilistic generation and requires mitigation strategies
  • Knowledge cutoff dates mean models lack awareness of recent events
  • Context window overflow loses earlier information without explicit management

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can explain the training pipeline from pre-training through RLHF.
  • Understands tokenization and its impact on model behavior and cost.
  • Knows the main failure modes of LLMs and mitigation approaches.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Assuming LLMs have reliable factual knowledge without verification.
  • Ignoring token costs when designing applications with large context needs.
  • Treating LLMs as deterministic systems rather than probabilistic generators.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How does RLHF differ from supervised fine-tuning?
  • What causes hallucination and how would you mitigate it in a production system?
  • Explain the tradeoff between context window size and inference cost.

Related topics

Keep the thread going

AI study companion

Go beyond the overview with practical examples

1 code example 1 design drill

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Can explain the training pipeline from pre-training through RLHF.
  • Understands tokenization and its impact on model behavior and cost.
  • Knows the main failure modes of LLMs and mitigation approaches.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Assuming LLMs have reliable factual knowledge without verification.
  • Ignoring token costs when designing applications with large context needs.
  • Treating LLMs as deterministic systems rather than probabilistic generators.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • How does RLHF differ from supervised fine-tuning?
  • What causes hallucination and how would you mitigate it in a production system?
  • Explain the tradeoff between context window size and inference cost.

Code example

Compare tokenization strategies

beginner coding

Tokenize the same text with different tokenizers (whitespace, BPE via tiktoken, character-level) and analyze the differences in vocabulary size, token count, and handling of rare words.

What to notice

  • tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base") gives GPT-4 tokenizer
  • enc.encode() returns token IDs, enc.decode([id]) shows the text
  • BPE balances vocabulary size with sequence length

Expected outcome

BPE produces fewer tokens than character-level but more than whitespace, handling subwords gracefully

Starter code 
# Install: pip install tiktoken
import tiktoken

text = "The transformer architecture revolutionized NLP. GPT-4's tokenizer handles subwords efficiently."

# Method 1: Whitespace tokenization
def whitespace_tokenize(text):
    return text.split()

# Method 2: Character-level
def char_tokenize(text):
    return list(text)

# Method 3: BPE (tiktoken)
def bpe_tokenize(text):
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
    # TODO: encode and decode to show tokens
    pass

# TODO: Compare token counts and show how each handles "GPT-4's"
# TODO: Calculate cost difference at $0.01 per 1K tokens
Reference solution 
import tiktoken

text = "The transformer architecture revolutionized NLP. GPT-4's tokenizer handles subwords efficiently."

def whitespace_tokenize(text):
    return text.split()

def char_tokenize(text):
    return list(text)

def bpe_tokenize(text):
    enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")
    tokens = enc.encode(text)
    decoded = [enc.decode([t]) for t in tokens]
    return tokens, decoded

ws_tokens = whitespace_tokenize(text)
char_tokens = char_tokenize(text)
bpe_ids, bpe_decoded = bpe_tokenize(text)

print(f"Whitespace: {len(ws_tokens)} tokens")
print(f"Character: {len(char_tokens)} tokens")
print(f"BPE: {len(bpe_ids)} tokens")
print(f"
BPE breakdown of 'GPT-4's': {[t for t in bpe_decoded if 'GPT' in t or '4' in t or "'" in t]}")

Design drill

Probe LLM limitations systematically

intermediate

Design a test suite that exposes common LLM failure modes: arithmetic errors, hallucination, inconsistency, and context window overflow.

Use these prompts to deepen the study pass

  • What arithmetic operations tend to fail and at what number sizes?
  • How would you test for factual hallucination in a verifiable domain?
  • Design a test that shows context window information loss
  • How would you measure inconsistency across repeated queries?

Interactive topic lab

LLM capabilities and limitations lab

Interactive diagram

LLM application architecture with grounding

Rendering diagram…

Ground model outputs in verified knowledge and add verification layers before serving to users.

Worked example

Ground LLM outputs in verified knowledge sources

A customer support bot answers questions about product features. Without grounding, it confidently states features that do not exist.

Recommended choice

Use RAG to inject verified product documentation into the context before generation.

Why it fits

  • The model generates from context, not memory, reducing hallucination risk.
  • Documentation updates are reflected immediately without retraining.
  • Citations can be traced back to source documents for verification.

If you chose differently

Fine-tuning on product docs embeds knowledge in weights but cannot be updated without retraining and may still hallucinate on edge cases.

Interview-ready takeaway

RAG plus citation verification gives the best tradeoff between accuracy and maintainability for factual Q&A systems.

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "LLM fundamentals" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • How does RLHF differ from supervised fine-tuning?
  • How LLMs are trained
  • Tokenization and context
  • Can explain the training pipeline from pre-training through RLHF.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants

Bring your own model
